Experiment 4: Human-AI Adaptation — Cross-Model Summary

Generated: 2026-03-30 12:26:17

1. Overview

This report extends the Gray, Gray, & Wegner (2007) mind perception paradigm from the original 13 entities to 30 characters (15 AI systems + 15 human characters). The Human-AI Adaptation branch tests whether LLMs systematically differentiate AI from human characters in the mind perception space.

Key questions:

  • Do LLMs assign different mental capacity profiles to AI vs human characters?
  • Does the two-factor (Experience/Agency) structure from Gray et al. emerge with 30 characters?
  • Is AI-human separation driven by descriptions or by names alone?

Stimuli

Characters

30 characters: 15 AI + 15 human. AI characters are identified by their descriptions as artificial systems. Human characters have naturalistic bios without explicit “is a human” labeling.

Mental Capacities (18 items)

Pairwise comparison items from Gray et al. (2007). Each completes: “Which character is more capable of …”

CapacityFactorQuestion
hungerExperiencewhich character is more capable of feeling hungry
fearExperiencewhich character is more capable of feeling afraid or fearful
painExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing physical or emotional pain
pleasureExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing physical or emotional pleasure
rageExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing violent or uncontrolled anger
desireExperiencewhich character is more capable of longing or hoping for things
personalityExperiencewhich character is more capable of having personality traits that make it unique from others
consciousnessExperiencewhich character is more capable of having experiences and being aware of things
prideExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing pride
embarrassmentExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing embarrassment
joyExperiencewhich character is more capable of experiencing joy
self_controlAgencywhich character is more capable of exercising self-restraint over desires, emotions, or impulses
moralityAgencywhich character is more capable of telling right from wrong and trying to do the right thing
memoryAgencywhich character is more capable of remembering things
emotion_recognitionAgencywhich character is more capable of understanding how others are feeling
planningAgencywhich character is more capable of making plans and working toward a goal
communicationAgencywhich character is more capable of conveying thoughts or feelings to others
thoughtAgencywhich character is more capable of thinking

3. Methods

  1. Pairwise comparisons: For each pair of characters and each of 18 mental capacities, the model judges which character is more capable. With 30 characters, this gives C(30,2) × 18 = 7,830 comparisons (fewer if some characters are excluded due to data issues).
  2. PCA with varimax rotation: Following Gray et al., we compute the correlation matrix across capacities, run PCA, retain factors with eigenvalue > 1 (minimum 2), and apply varimax rotation. Factor scores are rescaled to 0–1.
  3. AI-Human separation test: Mann-Whitney U tests compare mean factor scores for AI characters vs human characters on each factor.
  4. Names-only control (chat models): Repeats the pairwise paradigm using only character names (no descriptions), testing whether the model differentiates AI from human based on name alone.

Interpretation Guide

Methodology Primer

Pairwise Comparison Paradigm

In the pairwise paradigm, entities are compared two at a time on each mental capacity. The model is asked: “Which is more capable of X: Entity A or Entity B?” and rates on a 1–5 scale (1 = A clearly more, 5 = B clearly more, 3 = equal). Each pair is tested in both orders (A–B and B–A) to control for position bias.

Relative scores are computed by transforming pairwise ratings into per-entity win rates. For each entity–capacity combination, the relative score reflects how often and how strongly that entity was judged “more capable” than its comparison partners. These scores are aggregated into an entity × capacity matrix that serves as input to PCA.

PCA with Varimax Rotation

Principal Component Analysis (PCA) finds the axes of maximum variance in multivariate data. Applied to the entity–capacity correlation matrix, it reveals latent dimensions that organize mental capacity judgments.

Varimax rotation is applied after PCA extraction to achieve “simple structure”—each capacity loads strongly on one factor and weakly on others, making the factors more interpretable. The rotation does not change the total variance explained; it only redistributes it across factors. Gray et al. (2007) used this exact procedure and found two factors they labeled Experience (capacity for sensations and feelings) and Agency (capacity for planning, self-control, and communication).

Kaiser criterion: Only factors with eigenvalue > 1 are retained. A factor must account for more variance than a single original variable to be worth keeping. A minimum of 2 factors is enforced to allow comparison with the human 2-factor solution.

Factor scores are computed via the regression method and rescaled to 0–1, giving each entity a position in the rotated factor space. These coordinates form the “mind perception space” scatter plots.

Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ)

Spearman’s ρ measures the strength of a monotonic relationship between two ranked variables. It ranges from −1 (perfect inverse ranking) to +1 (identical ranking). It is preferred over Pearson’s r for this data because (a) mind perception scores may not be normally distributed, (b) the relationship between model and human scores need not be linear—only consistently ordered, and (c) rank correlation is robust to outliers.

Significance stars: * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001.

Chat vs. Base Model Prompting

Chat/instruct models receive prompts wrapped in their model-specific chat template (e.g., LLaMA-2 uses [INST] tags; LLaMA-3 uses <|start_header_id|> headers; Gemma-2 uses <start_of_turn>). The model generates a text response, and the numeric rating is extracted via regex.

Base models receive raw text prompts without chat formatting. Instead of generating a response, ratings are extracted from the next-token logit distribution over digit tokens. The expected rating is the probability-weighted sum: ∑d=1..5 d × P(d). This avoids requiring the base model to produce a formatted reply and provides a continuous rating value.

Why two methods? Chat models are trained to generate structured responses and reliably output numeric ratings. Base models lack instruction-following behavior and often fail to produce parseable responses, so logit-based extraction is more reliable. Both methods yield ratings on the same 1–5 scale.

Why 30 Characters?

The original 13 Gray et al. entities (baby, frog, robot, God, etc.) were designed to span the full mind perception space but include few modern AI systems. The 30-character set extends this to 15 AI characters (chatbots, virtual assistants, autonomous systems, robots) and 15 diverse human characters (varying in age, profession, background). This tests whether the Experience/Agency framework applies to entities the model has likely encountered in its training data, and whether models categorically separate AI from human characters.

Names-Only Condition

The names-only condition repeats the full pairwise comparison procedure using only character names (e.g., “Aria-7” or “David Park”) without descriptive bios. This tests whether the model differentiates AI from human based on name associations alone, revealing prior knowledge baked in during pretraining. If a model separates AI from human by name alone, it suggests the model has learned entity-type associations from its training corpus.

Anomalies

An anomaly is a character that loads on the unexpected side of the AI/human boundary in PCA space—e.g., an AI character clustering with humans (suggesting the model attributes human-like mental capacities to it), or a human character clustering with AI systems. Anomalies reveal which character features drive the model’s categorization beyond the AI/human label.

Statistical Tests for AI/Human Separation

AI vs. human group separation is tested with Mann–Whitney U tests comparing the mean PCA factor scores of the 15 AI characters vs. the 15 human characters on each factor. Separation is measured as the absolute difference in group means. A significant test (p < 0.05) indicates that factor reliably captures the AI/human distinction.

7. Anomalies

Characters that land on the “wrong side” of the AI-human divide: AI characters whose factor scores fall closer to the human group mean, or human characters whose scores fall closer to the AI group mean.

8. Names-Only Comparison

For models with names-only data, we repeat the pairwise paradigm using only character names (e.g., “ChatGPT” vs “Sam”) without descriptions. This tests whether AI-human separation is driven by the explicit description content or by name recognition alone.

9. Key Takeaways

  • LLaMA-2-13B Chat: 6-factor structure (eigenvalues: 8.6, 2.0, 1.6, 1.3, 1.2, 1.1). The mind perception space is more fragmented than the classic two-factor model.
  • LLaMA-2-13B Chat: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.021). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • LLaMA-2-13B Chat (names-only): No significant separation with names alone. Descriptions are necessary for the model to differentiate AI from human.
  • LLaMA-2-13B Base: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 16.5, 0.5), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • LLaMA-2-13B Base: Significant AI-human separation on F2 (p=0.001). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • LLaMA-3-8B Instruct: 3-factor structure (eigenvalues: 8.8, 4.3, 1.8). The mind perception space is more fragmented than the classic two-factor model.
  • LLaMA-3-8B Instruct: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • LLaMA-3-8B Base: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 16.5, 0.7), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • LLaMA-3-8B Base: Significant AI-human separation on F2 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Gemma-2-2B-IT: 4-factor structure (eigenvalues: 8.8, 3.7, 1.8, 1.3). The mind perception space is more fragmented than the classic two-factor model.
  • Gemma-2-2B-IT: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.002), F3 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Gemma-2-2B-IT (names-only): AI-human separation persists with names alone on F2. Name recognition alone is sufficient for differentiation.
  • Gemma-2-2B Base: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 16.2, 1.0), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • Gemma-2-2B Base: Significant AI-human separation on F2 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Gemma-2-2B Base (names-only): No significant separation with names alone. Descriptions are necessary for the model to differentiate AI from human.
  • Gemma-2-9B-IT: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 15.2, 1.8), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • Gemma-2-9B-IT: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Gemma-2-9B-IT (names-only): AI-human separation persists with names alone on F2, F3. Name recognition alone is sufficient for differentiation.
  • Gemma-2-9B Base: 3-factor structure (eigenvalues: 9.7, 4.9, 1.6). The mind perception space is more fragmented than the classic two-factor model.
  • Gemma-2-9B Base: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000), F2 (p=0.002). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Gemma-2-9B Base (names-only): No significant separation with names alone. Descriptions are necessary for the model to differentiate AI from human.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 12.5, 3.0), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000), F2 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B-Instruct (names-only): AI-human separation persists with names alone on F1, F3. Name recognition alone is sufficient for differentiation.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B Base: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 14.8, 2.0), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B Base: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Qwen-2.5-7B Base (names-only): AI-human separation persists with names alone on F1. Name recognition alone is sufficient for differentiation.
  • Qwen3-8B: Clean two-factor structure emerges (eigenvalues: 12.5, 3.7), mirroring the Experience/Agency dimensions from Gray et al.
  • Qwen3-8B: Significant AI-human separation on F1 (p=0.000). The model distinguishes AI from human characters in its mind perception judgments.
  • Qwen3-8B (names-only): AI-human separation persists with names alone on F2. Name recognition alone is sufficient for differentiation.
